Details: Other treatment processes that have been tested for the removal of tebuconazole in water include photolysis under natural sunlight with no catalyst [2145, 2146, 2147]; advanced oxidation processes (specifcally photocatalysis under natural sunlight) [2145, 2146, 2147]; and adsorption using natural vegetation substrate [2150]. One group of researchers found advanced oxidation processes to be very effective achieving greater than 99 percent removal of tebuconazole from leachate from an experimental greenhouse [2145, 2146]. The treatment using ZnO (photosensitizer) in the presence of Na2S2O8 under natural sunlight irradiation reduced a 500 μg/L tebuconazole influent to below detection limit [2146]. Another study confirmed the effectiveness of photocatalysis in degrading tebuconazole. Faster degradation and higher percent removal of tebuconazole were achieved under solar irridiation in the presence of a catalyst (Mo doped TiO2) and an oxidizing agent [(NH4)2S2O8] than under solar irridiation alone (93 versus 28 percent after 150-min reaction time) [2147]. Photolysis under natural sunlight alone (with no catalyst) was not effective in removing tebuconazole, 1 to 38 percent removal reported [2145, 2146]. The removal of tebuconazole from water through adsorption using hemp substrate was also not very effective. A 48 percent removal was achieved (influent = 20 μg/L) when the substrate was initially dry and only 20 percent was removed when the substrate was initially saturated with water [2150].
